featured.name: Archon
featured.tagline: Build AI agents that actually work, without writing glue code.
featured.why_radar: Agent frameworks keep shipping, but most are wrappers on wrappers. Archon takes a different line. It gives you composable blocks with proper state management and tool routing. We tested it with a multi-step research workflow and it held together. Worth watching.

featured.name: Luminal
featured.tagline: Clean, transform, and analyse spreadsheet data with natural language.
featured.why_radar: Natural language to pandas is not new. Luminal is interesting because it shows you the generated code before running it, lets you edit it, then builds a reusable pipeline. Good UX for the trust problem.

picks.name: PromptLayer 3.0
picks.tagline: Version control and observability for your prompts.
picks.why_radar: Prompt versioning is becoming table stakes. PromptLayer now has A/B testing built in. Useful if you are shipping prompts to production.

picks.name: Cline v3
picks.tagline: Autonomous AI coding agent that lives in your IDE.
picks.why_radar: The IDE-native agent race is heating up. Cline v3 adds multi-file context and checkpoint rollbacks. Getting closer to a real coding partner.

picks.name: Mem0
picks.tagline: Long-term memory layer for AI applications.
picks.why_radar: Memory is the missing piece in most agent stacks. Mem0 gives you a drop-in persistence layer that handles context windows gracefully. Simple API, fast retrieval.

picks.name: Dify 1.0
picks.tagline: Open-source platform for building AI workflows visually.
picks.why_radar: Dify has been solid for a while but 1.0 is a proper release. Visual workflow builder, RAG pipelines, and agent orchestration in one package. Open source, self-hostable.
